name: Maya Castellanos
subtitle: Twelve years guiding educators. Four hundred recorded practices. One unhurried voice — heard by almost two thousand schools every week.

pullQuote.text: A practice doesn't have to fix anything. It just has to give us somewhere honest to be, for five minutes.

qa.q: How did you come to mindfulness?
qa.a: Twenty-six third-graders, no air conditioning, and a colleague who slipped me a paperback. I lasted about a year before I started leading the practices myself.

qa.q: What does a recording morning look like?
qa.a: I'm up by four. Coffee, longhand script, no music. The mic is in our daughter's bedroom — best acoustics in the house. By 5:30 the freeway wakes up, so I have to be done.

qa.q: Hardest part of recording for kids?
qa.a: Not over-explaining. Kids don't need the why before they try something. They need a confident voice and a clear instruction. Trust comes faster than adults give them credit for.

qa.q: A practice you reach for on hard days?
qa.a: Three breaths, eyes closed, feet on the floor. That's it. It's almost embarrassing how often that's enough.

qa.q: What do you want kids to feel?
qa.a: Like there's nothing they have to do. The practice doesn't have to fix anything. It just has to give us somewhere honest to be, for five minutes.
